name: Indonesia
tagline: From the spice islands and the temples of Java to Southeast Asia's largest democracy.
summary: Indonesia is a vast archipelago of thousands of islands whose history stretches from early Austronesian seafarers through the great Hindu-Buddhist maritime realms of Srivijaya and Majapahit, the coming of Islam and the spice-trade sultanates, and more than three centuries of Portuguese and Dutch colonial intrusion centred on the Dutch East India Company. A national awakening in the early twentieth century, accelerated by the Japanese occupation of 1942-45, culminated in the Proclamation of Independence on 17 August 1945 by Sukarno and Mohammad Hatta. After a four-year revolution the Netherlands recognised Indonesian sovereignty in December 1949. The republic passed through Sukarno's Guided Democracy, the violent birth of Suharto's New Order amid the mass killings of 1965-66, and since 1998 has built one of the world's largest democracies.

founding.label: Proclamation of Independence
founding.detail: On the morning of 17 August 1945, days after Japan's surrender ended the Second World War, Sukarno and Mohammad Hatta proclaimed Indonesia an independent republic in Jakarta; the next day they were named president and vice-president. The Dutch sought to restore colonial rule, and only after the Indonesian National Revolution of 1945-1949 did the Netherlands formally recognise Indonesian sovereignty, transferring it on 27 December 1949.

quickFacts.label: Geography
quickFacts.value: World's largest archipelagic state, thousands of islands

quickFacts.label: Predominant religion
quickFacts.value: Islam; the world's most populous Muslim-majority nation

eras.title: Early Peoples and the Hindu-Buddhist Kingdoms: Srivijaya and Majapahit
eras.standfirst: Indianised maritime kingdoms rose across Sumatra and Java, from the Buddhist trading thalassocracy of Srivijaya to the imperial reach of Majapahit, leaving monuments such as Borobudur and Prambanan.
body: Long settled by Austronesian peoples, the western archipelago was drawn from the early first millennium CE into the trade and culture of the Indian Ocean. Indian religious and political ideas blended with local traditions to produce a succession of 'Indianised' Hindu and Buddhist kingdoms whose wealth rested on the monsoon trade in spices, gold and forest products passing through the straits between the Indian Ocean and the South China Sea.

From around the seventh century the Buddhist kingdom of Srivijaya, based at Palembang in southern Sumatra, dominated the maritime routes through the Straits of Malacca and Sunda, becoming a great centre of Buddhist learning. In central Java the Sailendra and Sanjaya dynasties raised two of the world's supreme religious monuments: the colossal Buddhist stupa of Borobudur, built around 780-850 CE, and the Hindu temple complex of Prambanan a little later.

Srivijaya's power waned from the eleventh century, eclipsed by Javanese states. In 1293 the kingdom of Majapahit arose in eastern Java and, under King Hayam Wuruk and his formidable chief minister Gajah Mada in the mid-fourteenth century, claimed influence over much of the archipelago, making it the most powerful of the pre-Islamic Indonesian realms. Majapahit declined through the fifteenth century as trade shifted and Islamic coastal states grew, but its memory became a touchstone of later Indonesian nationhood.

events.title: Rise of the Srivijaya thalassocracy
events.summary: From its base at Palembang in Sumatra, the Buddhist kingdom of Srivijaya comes to dominate maritime trade through the Straits of Malacca and Sunda and becomes a renowned centre of Buddhist scholarship.

events.title: Construction of Borobudur
events.summary: Under the Sailendra dynasty in central Java the great Buddhist monument of Borobudur is built; the world's largest Buddhist temple, it is today a UNESCO World Heritage Site.

events.title: Founding of the Majapahit empire
events.summary: Raden Wijaya establishes Majapahit in eastern Java, which grows into the most powerful Hindu-Buddhist empire of the archipelago.

events.title: Majapahit's golden age under Hayam Wuruk
events.summary: Under King Hayam Wuruk and his prime minister Gajah Mada, Majapahit reaches its height, projecting power and trade across much of the archipelago.

figures.name: Gajah Mada
figures.blurb: The powerful mahapatih (chief minister) under whom Majapahit reached its greatest extent. Tradition holds that he vowed not to rest until he had unified the archipelago, and he became a lasting symbol of Indonesian unity.

pullquote.text: Borobudur is the largest Buddhist monument in the world, a vast mandala in stone rising from the Javanese plain.
pullquote.attribution: On the Sailendra monument, c. 800 CE

eras.title: The Spread of Islam and the Spice-Trade Sultanates
eras.standfirst: Carried largely by traders along the sea lanes, Islam spread through the archipelago, and as Majapahit faded a constellation of Muslim sultanates grew rich on the spice trade.
body: Islam reached the archipelago chiefly through Muslim merchants from India, Arabia and beyond, who frequented its ports along the great trade routes. The faith took root first in the coastal trading towns of northern Sumatra from around the thirteenth century, then spread gradually and largely peaceably along the maritime networks, gathering pace through the fifteenth century as rulers and their courts converted.

As the inland Hindu-Buddhist order of Majapahit weakened, power passed to a string of Muslim coastal sultanates that thrived as entrepôts in the trade between India, China and the Spice Islands. On Java the Sultanate of Demak became a foothold from which Islam spread across the island; in Sumatra the Sultanate of Aceh rose to prominence, while in the Maluku islands the sultanates of Ternate and Tidore controlled the world's only sources of cloves and nutmeg.

By the end of the sixteenth century Islam had become the dominant religion of Java and Sumatra, woven into local cultures rather than replacing them wholesale. This patchwork of trading sultanates, immensely wealthy from spices coveted in Europe, was the world into which Portuguese and then Dutch ships would sail in search of direct control of the spice trade.

events.title: Islam takes root in northern Sumatra
events.summary: The early Muslim sultanate of Samudera Pasai in northern Sumatra marks the establishment of Islam in the archipelago, spread by traders along the maritime routes.

events.title: Rise of the Demak Sultanate on Java
events.summary: Demak emerges as the first major Islamic state on Java and a base from which Islam spreads across the island as Majapahit's power collapses.

events.title: Spice sultanates of Ternate, Tidore and Aceh flourish
events.summary: Muslim sultanates controlling the clove, nutmeg and pepper trade grow rich and powerful, making the archipelago the hub of a global spice economy.

eras.title: Portuguese and Dutch Colonialism and the VOC
eras.standfirst: Lured by spices, Europeans seized the trade by force. After the Portuguese took Malacca, the Dutch East India Company built a commercial empire on monopoly, violence and the near-extermination of the Banda islanders.
body: European intrusion began in 1511, when the Portuguese admiral Afonso de Albuquerque captured the great entrepôt of Malacca, gaining a foothold in the Asian spice trade. The Portuguese reached the Maluku 'Spice Islands' but never dominated the archipelago, and in the seventeenth century they were displaced by a more ruthless and better-organised competitor.

In 1602 the Dutch Republic merged its competing traders into the Vereenigde Oost-Indische Compagnie (VOC), the Dutch East India Company, granting it quasi-sovereign powers to wage war, build forts, mint coins and make treaties. From its headquarters at Batavia (modern Jakarta), founded by Governor-General Jan Pieterszoon Coen in 1619, the VOC fought to impose a monopoly over the spice trade.

The pursuit of that monopoly was brutal. In 1621 Coen ordered a campaign against the Banda Islands, the world's sole source of nutmeg and mace, in which the great majority of the roughly fifteen thousand Bandanese were killed, starved or enslaved and the survivors deported. This act of mass killing, undertaken to secure commercial control, stands as one of the most notorious atrocities of European expansion in Asia.

For nearly two centuries the VOC extended its grip through war, alliance and coercion, draining wealth from the islands. By the late eighteenth century, weighed down by corruption, war and debt, the company was bankrupt. It was dissolved in 1799 and its territories and obligations passed to the Dutch state, opening the era of direct colonial rule.

events.title: Portuguese capture of Malacca
events.summary: Afonso de Albuquerque seizes the trading city of Malacca, beginning sustained European involvement in the archipelago's spice trade.

events.title: Founding of the Dutch East India Company (VOC)
events.summary: The Dutch Republic charters the VOC with sweeping powers to trade, wage war and govern, launching nearly two centuries of company rule in the Indies.

events.title: Founding of Batavia
events.summary: Governor-General Jan Pieterszoon Coen storms Jayakarta and builds Batavia (modern Jakarta) as the VOC's Asian headquarters.

events.title: The Banda massacre
events.summary: To enforce the nutmeg monopoly, Coen's forces kill, starve, enslave or deport almost the entire population of the Banda Islands, an atrocity that destroys Bandanese society.

events.title: Dissolution of the VOC
events.summary: Bankrupt after decades of war, debt and corruption, the VOC is dissolved and its possessions pass to the Dutch state, ushering in direct colonial rule.

figures.name: Jan Pieterszoon Coen
figures.blurb: Architect of Dutch power in the Indies who founded Batavia and directed the 1621 destruction of the Banda Islands' population to secure the nutmeg monopoly. He remains a deeply controversial figure remembered for ruthless violence in the service of commerce.

pullquote.text: The almost total annihilation of the Bandanese was a clearly genocidal act committed to enforce the Dutch spice monopoly.
pullquote.attribution: On Coen's campaign in the Banda Islands, 1621

eras.title: The Dutch East Indies and the National Awakening
eras.standfirst: Under direct Dutch rule the islands became the Netherlands East Indies, exploited through forced cultivation; from this colonial order rose, in the early twentieth century, the first stirrings of Indonesian nationalism.
body: With the VOC gone, the archipelago became a colony governed directly from the Netherlands as the Dutch East Indies. From 1830 the colonial government imposed the Cultivation System (Cultuurstelsel), compelling Javanese peasants to devote land and labour to export crops such as coffee, sugar and indigo for the Dutch treasury. The system generated enormous profits for the Netherlands but brought hardship, dislocation and, in some districts, famine to the rural population.

Criticism of colonial exploitation, voiced in the Netherlands by works such as the novel Max Havelaar, helped prompt the early-twentieth-century 'Ethical Policy', which promised greater investment in welfare and education. Its expanded schooling, however, also produced a new educated Indonesian elite that began to question colonial rule itself.

Organised nationalism is often dated to 1908 and the founding of Budi Utomo, followed by the mass movement Sarekat Islam and, in 1927, Sukarno's Indonesian National Party. A defining moment came on 28 October 1928, when young activists proclaimed the Youth Pledge (Sumpah Pemuda), vowing allegiance to one motherland, one nation and one language, Indonesian. The Dutch responded with repression, imprisoning and exiling leaders, but a sense of a single Indonesian nation had taken hold.

events.title: The Cultivation System
events.summary: The Dutch impose the Cultuurstelsel, forcing Javanese peasants to grow export crops for the colonial state; it enriches the Netherlands while causing widespread hardship and local famines.

events.title: Founding of Budi Utomo
events.summary: The cultural society Budi Utomo is founded, widely regarded as the start of organised Indonesian nationalism and the National Awakening.

events.title: Sukarno founds the Indonesian National Party
events.summary: Sukarno establishes the PNI, demanding full independence and giving the nationalist movement a charismatic mass leader.

events.title: The Youth Pledge (Sumpah Pemuda)
events.summary: Young nationalists vow to recognise one Indonesian motherland, nation and language, a foundational moment in forging a unified national identity.

figures.name: Sukarno
figures.role: Nationalist leader and future first president
figures.blurb: Founder of the Indonesian National Party and the movement's pre-eminent orator. Repeatedly imprisoned and exiled by the Dutch, he articulated the ideology of a unified, independent Indonesia and would proclaim its independence in 1945.

eras.title: Japanese Occupation, Independence and the Revolution
eras.standfirst: The Japanese occupation shattered Dutch prestige and armed a generation of nationalists; the Proclamation of 17 August 1945 launched a four-year revolution that ended in recognised independence.
body: In 1942 Imperial Japan swept the Dutch from the Indies in weeks, ending more than three centuries of European control. The occupation was harsh: hundreds of thousands of Indonesians were conscripted as romusha forced labourers, many of whom died, and the population endured shortages and repression. Yet the Japanese, needing local support, also promoted Indonesian leaders such as Sukarno and Hatta and trained a militia, PETA, that would form the core of a future national army.

When Japan surrendered in August 1945, nationalist youth pressed Sukarno and Hatta to act at once. On the morning of 17 August 1945 the two men proclaimed Indonesia's independence in Jakarta, and the following day Sukarno became president and Hatta vice-president of the new republic, which adopted the Pancasila state philosophy and a constitution.

The Dutch refused to accept the loss of their colony and sought to return, triggering the Indonesian National Revolution. Years of guerrilla warfare, diplomacy and two major Dutch military offensives, euphemistically called 'police actions', followed, marked by violence on both sides. International pressure, especially from the United States and the United Nations, ultimately forced the Netherlands to negotiate.

At the Round Table Conference in The Hague, the Netherlands agreed to transfer sovereignty, and on 27 December 1949 it formally recognised the independence of Indonesia. The republic had won, through revolution and diplomacy, the freedom it had proclaimed four years earlier.

events.title: Japanese occupation of the Indies
events.summary: Japan conquers the Dutch East Indies, ending Dutch rule; its occupation brings forced labour and hardship but also mobilises and arms Indonesian nationalists.

events.title: Proclamation of Independence
events.summary: Sukarno and Mohammad Hatta proclaim Indonesia an independent republic in Jakarta, days after Japan's surrender; they become president and vice-president the next day.

events.title: The Indonesian National Revolution
events.summary: Indonesians wage a four-year armed and diplomatic struggle against Dutch attempts to reimpose colonial rule, enduring two major Dutch offensives and heavy losses.

events.title: Recognition of sovereignty
events.summary: Following the Round Table Conference in The Hague, the Netherlands formally transfers sovereignty and recognises the independence of Indonesia.

figures.name: Mohammad Hatta
figures.blurb: A Dutch-educated economist and nationalist who co-signed the 1945 Proclamation alongside Sukarno and served as the republic's first vice-president. He was a leading advocate of cooperative economics and constitutional government.

pullquote.text: We the people of Indonesia hereby declare the independence of Indonesia.

eras.title: Sukarno, the New Order and Democratic Indonesia
eras.standfirst: The young republic moved from Sukarno's Guided Democracy into the violent birth of Suharto's New Order amid the mass killings of 1965-66, and after three decades of authoritarian rule became, from 1998, a vigorous democracy.
body: After independence, parliamentary politics gave way in the late 1950s to Sukarno's 'Guided Democracy', an increasingly authoritarian and nationalist order that balanced the army, religious parties and the large Indonesian Communist Party (PKI). Economic decline and political tension mounted through the early 1960s.

On the night of 30 September 1965 an abortive coup, in which several senior generals were killed, was blamed on the PKI. The army, led by Major-General Suharto, used it to unleash a campaign of mass killing against suspected communists and others. Across late 1965 and 1966 an estimated five hundred thousand to more than a million people were murdered, often by soldiers and local militias, in one of the worst mass atrocities of the twentieth century, a crime that for decades went unacknowledged and remains largely unaddressed in Indonesia.

From the wreckage Suharto sidelined Sukarno and consolidated power, formally becoming president in 1968 and ruling for over three decades as the 'New Order'. His regime delivered economic growth and stability but rested on authoritarian control, military dominance, suppression of dissent and entrenched corruption, alongside the long and bloody occupation of East Timor from 1975.

The Asian financial crisis of 1997-98 shattered the New Order's economic foundations. Amid mass protests, Suharto resigned on 21 May 1998, opening the era of Reformasi. Since then Indonesia has decentralised power, held free multiparty elections, and grown into the world's third-largest democracy, while continuing to grapple with corruption, inequality and the unfinished reckoning with its violent past.

events.title: The anti-communist mass killings
events.summary: Following the 30 September coup attempt blamed on the PKI, an army-led purge kills an estimated 500,000 to over a million suspected communists and others, a vast atrocity that paves Suharto's path to power.

events.title: Suharto's New Order
events.summary: Suharto formally becomes president and rules for over three decades, delivering growth and stability through authoritarian control, military dominance and pervasive corruption.

events.title: Fall of Suharto and Reformasi
events.summary: Amid economic collapse and mass protests, Suharto resigns after 32 years in power, launching the Reformasi era of democratic reform and decentralisation.

events.title: First direct presidential election
events.summary: Indonesians directly elect their president for the first time, consolidating one of the world's largest democracies after the authoritarian New Order.

figures.name: Suharto
figures.role: Second president; leader of the New Order
figures.blurb: The general who oversaw the 1965-66 mass killings and supplanted Sukarno, ruling Indonesia from 1967-68 until 1998. His New Order brought economic development but also authoritarianism, military repression and large-scale corruption.

pullquote.text: At least 500,000 people were killed, the total may be as high as one million.
pullquote.attribution: On the Indonesian mass killings of 1965-66

figures.name: Sukarno
figures.blurb: Founder of Indonesian nationalism who proclaimed independence in 1945 and led the republic through revolution and Guided Democracy before being eclipsed by Suharto.

figures.name: Mohammad Hatta
figures.blurb: Co-proclaimer of independence and the republic's first vice-president, an economist remembered as a champion of constitutional and cooperative principles.

figures.name: Suharto
figures.blurb: Architect of the New Order whose rule began with the mass killings of 1965-66 and combined economic growth with authoritarianism and corruption.

sources.label: History of Indonesia (Islamic influence; Dutch rule)
Indonesia (toward independence; Japanese occupation; after Suharto)
Japanese occupation of the Dutch East Indies
Indonesia: US Documents Released on 1965-66 Massacres
